deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this patient.
Let me go through the case step by step.
The patient is a 41-year-old woman presenting with decreased visual acuity in the left eye and diplopia.
She had a sudden onset of a moderate frontal headache on the left side, which resolved after a few hours.
The next morning, she noticed numbness on the left side from her eyelid to her mouth and from her nose to her ear.
She went to the emergency department, and a CT scan of the head showed no abnormalities.
She was discharged, but the numbness persisted for two weeks.
Then, she developed ptosis and diplopia, with one image appearing diagonally over the other.
She went to another hospital where CT and CT angiography showed mucosal thickening in the ethmoid and sphenoid sinuses and air-liquid levels in the maxillary sinuses.
Blood work, including a complete blood count and metabolic panel, was normal.
An MRI with gadolinium showed changes consistent with sinusitis but was otherwise normal.
An ophthalmologist prescribed an eye patch for diplopia.
She had a lumbar puncture, which was negative for infection and showed no abnormalities.
She developed periorbital pain on the left side without other symptoms like fever or headache.
Diplopia recurred, and she had nausea, vomiting, and vertigo, falling due to it.
Her MRI at the second hospital showed soft tissue in the sphenoid sinus, orbital apex, and cavernous sinus.
Further imaging showed abnormal enhancement in the cavernous sinus and Meckel’s cave.
She had a large central scotoma on the left with normal peripheral vision.
Her MRI of the orbit showed soft tissue in the sphenoid and orbital apex.
Her medical history includes hypothyroidism, chronic rhinosinusitis, and allergies.
She had a past history of herpes zoster and a breast fibroadenoma.
She was on several medications, including ibuprofen for pain.
She doesn't have a history of fever or other systemic symptoms except for the periorbital pain and diplopia recurrence.
On physical exam, her temperature was slightly elevated at 37.4°C. Ophthalmologically, she had ptosis and diplopia, with decreased visual acuity in the left eye.
Pupils were reactive, but there was an afferent defect on the left.
MRI of the orbit showed soft tissue in the sphenoid and orbital apex, and CT showed intact bone despite the soft tissue.
Her CSF was normal, and tests for infections and autoimmune markers like anti-Ro antibodies were positive.
Urinalysis showed some ketones and trace blood, but no infection.
She was admitted for further evaluation.
Putting this together, the key findings are the MRI showing soft tissue in the sphenoid sinus, orbital apex, and cavernous sinus with enhancement.
The patient's symptoms include diplopia, ptosis, and periorbital pain.
The presence of anti-Ro antibodies suggests an autoimmune condition.
Given the MRI findings and the clinical picture, this seems like a case of autoimmune encephalomyelitis, specifically optic myelitis, which can be associated with sarcoidosis.
The orbital involvement and the presence of anti-Ro antibodies point towards this diagnosis.
Sarcoidosis can present with diplopia, ptosis, and orbital involvement, and it's associated with systemic symptoms like fatigue and fever, which she had a low-grade fever.
The orbital MRI changes and the enhancement in the cavernous sinus are also consistent with sarcoidosis.
